case_name: Bhagyashree Santosh Galande vs The Oriental Insurance Co Ltd on 21 October, 2024
court: MAHARASHTRA STATE CONSUMER DISPUTES REDRESSAL COMMISSION, MUMBAI, BENCH AT AURANGABAD
citation: First Appeal No. : 619 of 2022 in Complaint Case No.: 336 of 2021
compensation_amount: Rs. 2,50,000/- (sum insured) with interest @ 8% p.a. from 06.05.2021 till realization; plus Rs. 10,000/- for mental agony, financial harassment and litigation cost.

compensation_breakdown.final_amount: Rs. 2,50,000/- (insurance amount) with interest @ 8% p.a. from 06.05.2021 till realization; plus Rs. 10,000/- for mental agony, financial harassment and cost of litigation.
compensation_breakdown.notes: District Commission's dismissal quashed; State Commission partly allowed appeal and directed payment.

facts: The appellant had taken a 'Corona Rakshak Policy' (policy no. 182100/48/2021/4321) valid 22.09.2020 to 03.07.2021 and paid premium Rs.2,580/-. As per RTPCR report appellant tested COVID-19 positive on 09.03.2021 and was admitted to the MIT Boys Hostel COVID centre (declared by Aurangabad Municipal Corporation) from 09.03.2021 to 16.03.2021. The appellant informed respondent no.1 on 09.03.2021, filed a claim on 19.03.2021 which respondent no.1 repudiated on 06.05.2021 citing policy exclusion clause 3.6. The appellant filed a consumer complaint claiming Rs.2,50,000/- (sum insured) with interest and additional amounts; the District Commission dismissed the complaint and the appellant appealed.
arguments_claimant: The claimant argued that the policy requires a positive COVID diagnosis and hospitalization for more than 72 hours in a government hospital or a hospital established under local authority; the appellant was COVID positive and hospitalized for eight days in a COVID centre duly approved by the Municipal Corporation, hence eligible for the benefit. It was submitted that the District Commission failed to properly consider policy clauses (clause 2, clause 4.1, clause 3.6) and the documentary evidence (RTPCR report, institutional list, discharge card).
arguments_respondent: Respondent no.1 contended that the treatment recorded in the discharge card was simple with no active line of treatment and thus there was no need for hospitalization; the COVID centre was a quarantine centre and not a hospital, invoking policy exclusion clause 3.6/3.7 to justify repudiation. The insurer maintained there was no deficiency in service in repudiating the claim.
ratio_decidendi: "For the purpose of this policy any other set-up designated by the government as hospital for the treatment of Covid-19 shall also be considered as hospital." The court held that where the government declared a set-up (here MIT Boys Hostel) as a Covid centre having medical practitioners and nursing staff, such a set-up "comes within the purview of clause 3.6 (vi) of policy". Applying the operative clause and Clause 4.1 (Covid cover), the court found the appellant was "Corona positive and hospitalized for more than 72 hours as per the advice of qualified medical practitioner" and therefore "entitled for benefits under the Corona Rakshak Policy." Consequently, repudiation on the ground that the centre was not a hospital was held to be a deficiency in service by respondent no.1.
final_order: The appeal is partly allowed; the District Commission order in C.C.No.336/2021 dated 26.07.2022 is quashed and set aside. Respondent no.1 is directed to pay Rs.2,50,000/- under the Corona Rakshak Policy with interest @8% p.a. from 06.05.2021 till realization, and Rs.10,000/- for mental agony, financial harassment and litigation cost.
summary: This is a consumer appeal against Oriental Insurance's repudiation of a COVID insurance claim. The legal issue was whether a government-declared COVID centre qualifies as a 'hospital' under the policy (clause 3.6/3.7) and whether the appellant met the >72-hour hospitalization requirement. The State Commission held that government-designated COVID centres are covered and that the appellant satisfied the policy conditions, ordering payment of the sum insured with interest and a small amount for harassment. Practically, insurers cannot deny COVID policy claims solely because treatment occurred in government-designated COVID centres.
embedding_summary: Maharashtra State Consumer Commission (Aurangabad), First Appeal No.619 of 2022 (C.C.336/2021), judgment 21/10/2024: appellant insured under Corona Rakshak Policy (policy no.182100/48/2021/4321; premium Rs.2,580) tested RTPCR positive on 09.03.2021 and was admitted to MIT Boys Hostel COVID centre (09.03.2021-16.03.2021). Insurer (The Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd.) repudiated claim on 06.05.2021 citing clause 3.6/3.7 (definition of hospital/hospitalization). Key legal issue: interpretation of clause 3.6(vi) and Clause 4.1 (Covid cover) — whether government-designated COVID centres are 'hospitals' and whether hospitalization >72 hours was satisfied. Outcome: appeal partly allowed; insurer directed to pay Rs.2,50,000/- (sum insured) with interest @8% p.a. from 06.05.2021 and Rs.10,000/- for mental agony and litigation costs. Legal doctrines: clause 3.6(vi) interpretation, entitlement where RTPCR positive and >72 hours hospitalization in government-authorized centre, repudiation = deficiency in service.
legal_principles: Government-designated COVID centres qualify as 'hospital' under clause 3.6(vi) of the Corona Rakshak Policy
Where insured is COVID positive (government-authorized diagnostic) and hospitalized for more than 72 continuous hours on medical advice, Clause 4.1 operative benefit is payable
Repudiation of claim on ground that a government-designated COVID centre is only a quarantine centre is improper and constitutes deficiency in service
Interest awarded on repudiated insurance amount (here 8% p.a. from date of repudiation until realization)
Appellate forum will quash orders which fail to properly appreciate documentary medical evidence (RTPCR, lists, discharge card)
sections_cited: Clause 2 (operative clause) of Corona Rakshak Policy
Clause 3.6 (definition of 'hospital') of Corona Rakshak Policy
Clause 3.7 (hospitalization definition) of Corona Rakshak Policy
Clause 4.1 (Covid cover) of Corona Rakshak Policy
bm25_text: Bhagyashree Santosh Galande vs The Oriental Insurance Co Ltd on 21 October, 2024 MAHARASHTRA STATE CONSUMER DISPUTES REDRESSAL COMMISSION, MUMBAI, BENCH AT AURANGABAD 2024 The appellant had taken a 'Corona Rakshak Policy' (policy no. 182100/48/2021/4321) valid 22.09.2020 to 03.07.2021 and paid premium Rs.2,580/-. As per RTPCR report appellant tested COVID-19 positive on 09.03.2021 and was admitted to the MIT Boys Hostel COVID centre (declared by Aurangabad Municipal Corporation) from 09.03.2021 to 16.03.2021. The appellant informed respondent no.1 on 09.03.2021, filed a claim on 19.03.2021 which respondent no.1 repudiated on 06.05.2021 citing policy exclusion clause 3.6. The appellant filed a consumer complaint claiming Rs.2,50,000/- (sum insured) with interest and additional amounts; the District Commission dismissed the complaint and the appellant appealed. The claimant argued that the policy requires a positive COVID diagnosis and hospitalization for more than 72 hours in a government hospital or a hospital established under local authority; the appellant was COVID positive and hospitalized for eight days in a COVID centre duly approved by the Municipal Corporation, hence eligible for the benefit. It was submitted that the District Commission failed to properly consider policy clauses (clause 2, clause 4.1, clause 3.6) and the documentary evidence (RTPCR report, institutional list, discharge card). Respondent no.1 contended that the treatment recorded in the discharge card was simple with no active line of treatment and thus there was no need for hospitalization; the COVID centre was a quarantine centre and not a hospital, invoking policy exclusion clause 3.6/3.7 to justify repudiation. The insurer maintained there was no deficiency in service in repudiating the claim. "For the purpose of this policy any other set-up designated by the government as hospital for the treatment of Covid-19 shall also be considered as hospital." The court held that where the government declared a set-up (here MIT Boys Hostel) as a Covid centre having medical practitioners and nursing staff, such a set-up "comes within the purview of clause 3.6 (vi) of policy". Applying the operative clause and Clause 4.1 (Covid cover), the court found the appellant was "Corona positive and hospitalized for more than 72 hours as per the advice of qualified medical practitioner" and therefore "entitled for benefits under the Corona Rakshak Policy." Consequently, repudiation on the ground that the centre was not a hospital was held to be a deficiency in service by respondent no.1. The appeal is partly allowed; the District Commission order in C.C.No.336/2021 dated 26.07.2022 is quashed and set aside. Respondent no.1 is directed to pay Rs.2,50,000/- under the Corona Rakshak Policy with interest @8% p.a. from 06.05.2021 till realization, and Rs.10,000/- for mental agony, financial harassment and litigation cost. This is a consumer appeal against Oriental Insurance's repudiation of a COVID insurance claim. The legal issue was whether a government-declared COVID centre qualifies as a 'hospital' under the policy (clause 3.6/3.7) and whether the appellant met the >72-hour hospitalization requirement. The State Commission held that government-designated COVID centres are covered and that the appellant satisfied the policy conditions, ordering payment of the sum insured with interest and a small amount for harassment. Practically, insurers cannot deny COVID policy claims solely because treatment occurred in government-designated COVID centres. Maharashtra State Consumer Commission (Aurangabad), First Appeal No.619 of 2022 (C.C.336/2021), judgment 21/10/2024: appellant insured under Corona Rakshak Policy (policy no.182100/48/2021/4321; premium Rs.2,580) tested RTPCR positive on 09.03.2021 and was admitted to MIT Boys Hostel COVID centre (09.03.2021-16.03.2021). Insurer (The Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd.) repudiated claim on 06.05.2021 citing clause 3.6/3.7 (definition of hospital/hospitalization). Key legal issue: interpretation of clause 3.6(vi) and Clause 4.1 (Covid cover) — whether government-designated COVID centres are 'hospitals' and whether hospitalization >72 hours was satisfied. Outcome: appeal partly allowed; insurer directed to pay Rs.2,50,000/- (sum insured) with interest @8% p.a. from 06.05.2021 and Rs.10,000/- for mental agony and litigation costs. Legal doctrines: clause 3.6(vi) interpretation, entitlement where RTPCR positive and >72 hours hospitalization in government-authorized centre, repudiation = deficiency in service. Jayashree w/o Satish Kharote (appellant) Rs. 2,50,000/- (insurance amount) with interest @ 8% p.a. from 06.05.2021 till realization; plus Rs. 10,000/- for mental agony, financial harassment and cost of litigation. 8% p.a. from 06.05.2021 till realization District Commission's dismissal quashed; State Commission partly allowed appeal and directed payment.
